coredata.dc:description: Doxorubicin (DOX) is a highly effective chemotherapeutic agent extensively utilized in treating numerous cancers. However, its clinical efficacy is often limited by issues related to poor solubility, non-specific distribution, and dose-dependent adverse effects. To address these limitations, nanotechnology-based drug delivery systems have provided a compelling avenue to enhance the therapeutic index of doxorubicin and minimize off-target toxicity. Herein, a nanocarrier system composed of chitosan (CS), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) was developed through the double nanoemulsion (W/O/W) technique for improving the anticancer efficacy of DOX. FT-IR and XRD analyses were conducted to characterize the nanocomposite, confirming the presence of all components and providing insights into intermolecular interactions and crystallinity, respectively. FE-SEM and DLS analyses revealed that the DOX-loaded CS-PVP-CQD nanocarriers exhibited uniform dispersion, spherical shape, and an average diameter of 218 nm, suitable for drug delivery applications. The drug loading capacity of the developed nanocarrier system exhibited exceptional results, with a loading efficiency of 47.75 %±2.17 and an entrapment efficiency of 86.42 %±1.39. In vitro drug release studies demonstrated sustained and pH-sensitive release of DOX over 96 h, with 92.25 %±0.9 and 60 %±1.5 release under mimicking the tumor microenvironment (pH 5.4) and simulating physiological pH (pH 7.4), respectively, ensuring targeted drug delivery while minimizing premature release during circulation. The nanocarrier's ability to induce programmed cell death in breast cancer cells was investigated through cytotoxicity and apoptosis assays. Results revealed that the sustained release of DOX, facilitated by the nanocarrier, effectively triggered apoptosis, demonstrating a potential therapeutic advantage. These findings underscore the importance of DOX-nanocarrier interactions in achieving the desired therapeutic outcomes. This multifunctional nanocarrier system offers improved drug-loading efficiency, controlled release kinetics, and targeted delivery to tumor tissues, thereby enhancing therapeutic efficacy while minimizing off-target toxicity. These findings pave the way for promising translational research and development, with the ultimate goal of translating this nanocarrier system into clinical applications for the benefit of cancer patients, ultimately leading to improved treatment outcomes and a better quality of life.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Doxorubicin (DOX) is a highly effective chemotherapeutic agent extensively utilized in treating numerous cancers. However, its clinical efficacy is often limited by issues related to poor solubility, non-specific distribution, and dose-dependent adverse effects. To address these limitations, nanotechnology-based drug delivery systems have provided a compelling avenue to enhance the therapeutic index of doxorubicin and minimize off-target toxicity. Herein, a nanocarrier system composed of chitosan (CS), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) was developed through the double nanoemulsion (W/O/W) technique for improving the anticancer efficacy of DOX. FT-IR and XRD analyses were conducted to characterize the nanocomposite, confirming the presence of all components and providing insights into intermolecular interactions and crystallinity, respectively. FE-SEM and DLS analyses revealed that the DOX-loaded CS-PVP-CQD nanocarriers exhibited uniform dispersion, spherical shape, and an average diameter of 218 nm, suitable for drug delivery applications. The drug loading capacity of the developed nanocarrier system exhibited exceptional results, with a loading efficiency of 47.75 %±2.17 and an entrapment efficiency of 86.42 %±1.39. In vitro drug release studies demonstrated sustained and pH-sensitive release of DOX over 96 h, with 92.25 %±0.9 and 60 %±1.5 release under mimicking the tumor microenvironment (pH 5.4) and simulating physiological pH (pH 7.4), respectively, ensuring targeted drug delivery while minimizing premature release during circulation. The nanocarrier's ability to induce programmed cell death in breast cancer cells was investigated through cytotoxicity and apoptosis assays. Results revealed that the sustained release of DOX, facilitated by the nanocarrier, effectively triggered apoptosis, demonstrating a potential therapeutic advantage. These findings underscore the importance of DOX-nanocarrier interactions in achieving the desired therapeutic outcomes. This multifunctional nanocarrier system offers improved drug-loading efficiency, controlled release kinetics, and targeted delivery to tumor tissues, thereby enhancing therapeutic efficacy while minimizing off-target toxicity. These findings pave the way for promising translational research and development, with the ultimate goal of translating this nanocarrier system into clinical applications for the benefit of cancer patients, ultimately leading to improved treatment outcomes and a better quality of life.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer stands out among the most prevalent cancers that pose a significant health burden worldwide. Chemotherapy, as a systemic treatment modality, has been widely utilized in the management of breast cancer to eradicate cancer cells and prevent disease recurrence (Tagde et al., 2022; Rajaei et al., 2023). While chemotherapy has demonstrated efficacy in improving survival rates and overall patient outcomes, it has several drawbacks (Dubey et al., 2022). A significant limitation of chemotherapy is its non-specific targeting, as it affects both cancerous and healthy cells, resulting in a range of undesirable side effects (Alizadeh et al., 2023). Additionally, chemotherapy resistance can develop over time, leading to treatment failure and disease progression (Sun et al., 2023). This resistance can arise from various mechanisms, including alterations in drug targets, increased drug efflux, and enhanced DNA repair capabilities (Kinnel et al., 2023). Therefore, there is a critical need to explore and development of alternative strategies that can improve therapeutic efficacy while minimizing toxicity and drug resistance.

The requirement for multiple cycles of chemotherapy over an extended duration frequently results in a substantial deterioration of patients' quality of life. The physical and psychological burden associated with chemotherapy can result in treatment discontinuation or suboptimal adherence, which can compromise treatment outcomes (Kuderer et al., 2022; Cucciniello et al., 2023). To address these drawbacks, ongoing research focuses on combination therapies that combine chemotherapy with targeted agents to enhance treatment response and overcome resistance (Yu et al., 2022; Ashrafizadeh et al., 2023). Considerable research efforts within oncology have been dedicated to the design and development of targeted drug delivery systems for cancer therapy (Pourmadadi et al., 2023). Traditional cancer treatments, such as chemotherapy, often suffer from limitations such as systemic toxicity and poor targeting of tumor cells. The complexity and heterogeneity of cancer necessitate targeted and efficient therapeutic agent delivery to tumor sites while minimizing adverse effects on healthy tissues (Kong et al., 2023). Nanoparticle-based systems have attracted considerable attention because of enhanced drug delivery efficiency, specifically targeting tumor sites while minimizing off-target effects. These systems can encapsulate chemotherapy drugs, immunotherapeutics, or gene therapies, providing controlled release and protection of the payload. Additionally, surface modifications and targeting ligands can be incorporated to improve tumor specificity and enhance cellular uptake (Elumalai et al., 2024). In this regard, natural polymeric nanoparticles (NPs) hold promise platforms for drug delivery because of their biocompatibility and favorable solubility in aqueous environments. These properties can contribute to reducing side effects and enhance the bioavailability of drugs during the chemotherapy process.

CS, a natural polysaccharide derived from chitin, is biocompatible, biodegradable, and non-toxic, holds great potential for use in drug delivery applications (Pathak et al., 2023). The utilization of CS NPs for drug delivery offers several advantages. Firstly, CS NPs could be easily synthesized through various methods, including ionotropic gelation (Gonçalves et al., 2023), coacervation (Zhang et al., 2023), and emulsion (Tenorio-Barajas et al., 2023) methods. These techniques allow for encapsulating both hydrophilic and hydrophobic drugs within the chitosan matrix (Guadarrama-Escobar et al., 2023). Besides, CS NPs protect drugs against degradation, enzymatic degradation, and premature release, thus increasing their stability and bioavailability. The positively charged amino groups present in chitosan NPs facilitate mucoadhesion, enabling them to adhere to mucosal surfaces and enhance drug absorption (Liu et al., 2019; Kim et al., 2023). The cationic nature of CS facilitates electrostatic interactions with the anionic cell membrane, resulting in improved cellular uptake of loaded drug (Zhao et al., 2020).

CQDs possess excellent biocompatibility, making them suitable for use in biomedical applications. They exhibit low cytotoxicity and can be easily functionalized to improve their biocompatibility and stability (Sengupta et al., 2023; Ghanbarlou et al., 2025) (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2025.128176). The significant surface area-to-volume ratio of CQDs facilitates considerable drug loading (González-Reyna et al., 2023). Their small size allows for efficient cellular uptake, enhancing targeted drug delivery. The encapsulation properties of CQDs extend to both hydrophobic and hydrophilic therapeutic agents, rendering them appropriate for various therapeutic agents (Karami et al., 2023). Moreover, CQDs have tunable optical properties, including fluorescence, which enables real-time imaging and tracking of drug release and distribution. This feature is particularly valuable for monitoring drug delivery in vivo and optimizing treatment regimens (Shukla et al., 2023). Apart from delivering drugs, CQDs can also act as therapeutic agents themselves. They have demonstrated promise for photodynamic therapy, where their fluorescence properties could be harnessed to generate reactive oxygen species for targeted cancer treatment (Jovanović et al., 2023).

New drug delivery systems can be optimized for different anticancer drugs to both increase drug delivery efficiency and reduce side effects. Chatterjee et al. (Chatterjee et al., 2025) prepared a chitosan-based nanocarrier containing a Fe3O4 core containing the drug 5-FU by co-precipitation to investigate its anticancer properties. The nanocarrier exhibits supermagnetic properties that are very useful for achieving precise and regulated drug delivery. Loading efficiency was reported to be between 85 and 90 % (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.139246). Hamrang et al. (Hamrang et al., 2023) synthesized a drug delivery system using chitosan, folic acid, and the cytarabine drug to investigate its anticancer properties. They reported that cytarabine release rates at pH 5.6 and 7.4 were 99 % and 61 %, respectively (https://doi.org/10.1002/bab.2355). The ability of PVP to enhance drug stability and bioavailability, alongside its versatility, has established it as an important component in various drug delivery formulations (Luo et al., 2021). A key application of PVP in drug delivery systems lies in its ability to enhance drug solubility, improving their absorption and bioavailability (Loo et al., 2023). Prabha et al. (Prabha & Raj, 2016) synthesized a chitosan-PVP-based nanocarrier containing Fe3O4 nanoparticles to load curcumin. The use of PVP with amphiphilic properties resulted in better solubility of curcumin and improved drug delivery efficiency (https://doi.org/10.1002/jbm.b.33637). Many drugs have poor solubility in water, which can limit their absorption and therapeutic efficacy. By forming inclusion complexes with hydrophobic drugs, PVP can significantly improve their solubility and dissolution rate, ensuring better drug release and absorption in the body (Khan et al., 2023). In addition to its solubilizing properties, PVP also acts as a stabilizer in drug delivery systems. It can prevent drug degradation, such as oxidation or hydrolysis, by forming a protective barrier around the drug molecules. This helps to maintain the drug's potency and integrity during transportation and storage, ensuring its effectiveness when administered to patients (Contardi et al., 2019). Furthermore, PVP is commonly used in developing controlled-release drug delivery systems (Kurakula & Rao, 2020). By incorporating PVP into the matrix of the delivery system, it can modulate the drug's release kinetics. PVP can control the diffusion rate and dissolution behavior of the drug, providing sustained and controlled release, thereby optimizing the pharmacokinetic profile for drugs requiring prolonged therapeutic efficacy (Samadi et al., 2021).

This study aimed to design a biocompatible hydrogel nanocarrier composed of CS, PVP, and CQDs for targeted drug delivery applications. The nanocarrier was loaded with the DOX through the water-in-oil emulsification technique. The purpose of selecting CQDs was to improve drug loading and absorption into the nanocomposite. The CS-PVP-CQDs@ DOX platform was characterized using FTIR, XRD, FE-SEM, and DLS. Drug loading efficiency and release kinetics were assessed under both tumor-mimicking and physiological conditions. The efficacy of the developed platform against MCF-7 human breast cancer cells was determined via MTT assay, with the mechanism of cell death induction further investigated using flow cytometry. The findings showed that the designed nanoplatforms effectively released DOX under pH stimulation and specifically targeted the tumor site.

Low molecular weight chitosan (LMW CS, 50–190 kDa, degree of deacetylation 75 %–85 %), di-ammonium hydrogen citrate, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), SPAN 80, and 12 kDa dialysis membranes were obtained from Merck (Germany). Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP K90, 1200 KDa) and doxorubicin (DOX) were supplied from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Bitter almond oil (food grade) was sourced locally. Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), trypsin, and penicillin/streptomycin were sourced from Beijing Solarbio Science and Technology (China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was obtained from Gibco Life Technologies, Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM, High Glucose) from HyClone Thermo Scientific, and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (USA). The MCF-7 (human breast cancer) cell lines were sourced from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA). All reagents were analytical grade and used without additional purification. The preparation of all solutions involved distilled water (18.2 MΩ cm) purified by a Milli-Q system (PureFlow Inc.).

CQDs were synthesized using a modified hydrothermal method adapted from Ostovar et al. (2023). Briefly, 2 g of diammonium hydrogen citrate was dissolved in 75 ml of deionized water and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 120 °C for 10 h in a 150 ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Upon cooling to room temperature, the CQDs were collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The resulting supernatant was discarded, and the CQD pellet was washed 3 times and re-centrifuged. The resulting CQDs, with a concentration of 27.1 mg.ml−1, were characterized for further use in nanocarrier synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and DOX loading of CS-PVP-CQDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of DOX-loaded CS-PVP-CQD nanocarriers involved a two-step process: hydrogel formation followed by emulsification and isolation.

Hydrogel Formation: A 2 % (w/v) chitosan (CS) solution was prepared by dissolving 1 g of CS in 50 ml of 2 % (v/v) acetic acid under constant magnetic stirring at room temperature until complete dissolution was achieved. Then add 0.5 g of PVP to the above solution until it becomes homogeneous by stirring. Subsequently, CQDs (50 mg) were introduced into the solution and allowed to dissolve completely. The solution is then subjected to ultrasonication for 10 min to ensure homogenous distribution. Following this, DOX was incorporated into the CS-PVP-CQD solution at a final concentration of 5 µg. ml−1. The mixture was subjected to continuous stirring on a heater stirrer for 30 min to ensure uniform DOX dispersion and facilitate hydrogel formation.

Emulsification and Nanocarrier Isolation: To encapsulate the DOX-loaded CS-PVP-CQD hydrogel, 0.2 % (v/v) Span 80 was incorporated into the hydrogel, which was then dropwise added to 15 ml of vigorously stirred bitter almond oil. This process continues until the formation of spherical-shaped nanocarriers containing DOX within the hydrophobic phase. Following 10 min, 15 ml of deionized water was introduced dropwise to the emulsion, maintaining constant stirring for 15 min. The emulsion was allowed to stand undisturbed for 5 min to allow for phase separation. The drug-loaded nanocarriers were isolated from the aqueous phase through centrifugation at 4500 rpm for 15 min. The isolated drug-loaded nanocarriers were subsequently lyophilized and stored at −20 °C for further use (Fig. 1).

To determine the entrapment and loading efficiency of DOX within the CS-PVP-CQD nanocarriers, a quantitative analysis was performed using a solvent extraction method. Specifically, 1 mg of the CS-PVP-CQD@DOX nanocomposite was accurately weighed and dispersed in 1 ml of PBS. 1 ml of ethyl acetate was then introduced to the solution, followed by vigorous shaking to ensure complete extraction. The content of free DOX present in the ethyl acetate phase was then quantified at 485 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Itoo et al., 2023). The entrapment and loading efficiencies were subsequently calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively.

Where 'Total amount of DOX' represents the initial amount of DOX added during nanocarrier preparation, and 'Free amount of DOX' is the amount of DOX determined spectrophotometrically in the ethyl acetate phase after extraction.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was performed to characterize the chemical structures of the CS, CQDs, PVP, CS-PVP, CS-PVP-CQDs nanocomposite, and CS-PVP-CQDs@ DOX nanocarrier. Spectra were recorded at 25 °C on KBr pellets within the 600–4000 cm−1 wavenumber range. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted at room temperature to determine the crystalline structure of powdered forms of CS, CQDs, PVP, and their composites using a Philips PW1730 diffractometer with a Cu Kα radiation source (2θ = 10–80°). The surface morphology of the synthesized nanocarriers was investigated using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) with a Tescan MIRA III microscope.

To determine the in vitro drug release profile of DOX from the CS-PVP-CQD nanocarriers, a dialysis bag diffusion method was employed. Two different PBS solutions, adjusted to pH 5.4 (simulating the acidic tumor microenvironment) and pH 7.4 (simulating physiological pH), were used as release media, within a 37 °C water bath. A 1 ml aliquot of the CS-PVP-CQDs@ DOX nanocarrier solution was introduced into a dialysis bag (MWCO 3.5 kDa). The dialysis bag was then immersed in 15 ml of the respective PBS solution containing 20 % (v/v) ethanol to enhance DOX solubility. At predetermined time intervals, 300 µL samples were collected from the release medium and immediately replaced with an equal volume of fresh PBS solution. The concentration of released DOX in the buffer was determined spectrophotometrically at 485 nm (Rahmani et al., 2022). The amount of DOX released was calculated using the following equation:

Where, [DOX]load denotes the DOX encapsulated in the nanocarrier, while [DOX]rel indicates the amount released.

The MCF-7 breast cancer cells were cultured in high-glucose DMEM supplemented with 1 % penicillin-streptomycin and 10 % FBS, incubated under standard cell culture conditions (humidified atmosphere, 5 % CO2, 37 °C). To evaluate the cytotoxicity of various formulations including CS, CS-PVP, CS-CQD-PVP, and free drugs, an MTT assay was performed. All drug-containing formulations were prepared at an equivalent drug concentration of 4.8 µg. ml−1. Cells (5 × 10³ cells per well) were seeded in a 96-well plate and allowed to attach during a 24-hour incubation, reaching a confluency of over 80 %. After this initial incubation, cells were treated with the aforementioned groups and further incubated for 48 h. Untreated cells served as a control. Following 48-hour incubation, each well was treated with 20 µl of MTT reagent (5 mg.ml−1 in PBS, pH 7.4) and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. Following removal of medium, the formazan crystals were dissolved in 200 µL DMSO. A microplate reader was used to measure absorbance at 570 nm after 10 min of gentle shaking. Cell viability was determined using the following equation:

The developed nanocomposites' effects on MCF-7 cell apoptosis and necrosis were studied through flow cytometry analysis with a BD Biosciences flow cytometer and an Annexin V kit. Following a 24-hour incubation in a 6-well plate at a density of 5 × 10³ cells per well, breast cancer cells were subsequently treated with the CS-PVP-CQDs@ DOX nanocarrier, CS-PVP-CQD nanocomposite, CS-PVP, CS, and free drug in PBS medium with a pH of 7.4 (Zavareh et al., 2020).

All experiments were conducted in triplicate to ensure reliability and consistency. Data was analyzed by presenting the findings as mean values ± SD. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to assess statistical significance (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001).

FTIR technique, employed as elemental analysis, serves as a valuable tool for the identification and characterization of functional groups, as well as atomic and intermolecular stretching vibrations (Siddiqa et al., 2023). To verify the presence of all constituents within the nanocarrier and gain insights into their interactions, FTIR spectra were recorded. Additionally, the successful synthesis of CQDs was confirmed. Fig. 2 illustrates the FTIR spectrum of PVP, CS, CQDs, CS-PVP, CS-PVP-CQDs, and CS-PVP-CQDs@ DOX. Analysis of the FTIR pattern of CS revealed four distinct peaks. The broad peak observed at approximately 3350 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibrations associated with both NH2 and –OH functional groups (Pourmadadi et al., 2023). The absorption band around 2856 cm−1 was associated with stretching vibration of -C-H (Rabie et al., 2023). A peak at 1584 cm−1 could indicate C = O stretching vibrations, while a band at 1020 cm−1 suggests C–O–C stretching (Nematollahi et al., 2021). An absorption band at 887 cm⁻¹ could be consistent with out-of-plane bending of CH bonds in monosaccharide rings.

In the spectrum of CQDs, a prominent band at 3444 cm−1, indicates NH and OH stretching vibrations. Further peaks were observed at 1385 cm⁻¹ (OH bending), 1631 cm⁻¹ (CO-C stretching), 1490 cm⁻¹ (CH₂ bending or stretching), and 1117 cm⁻¹ (CN stretching) (Yang et al., 2021). These bands are consistent with the presence of hydroxyl, amine, and CO functional groups, corroborating previous findings. Furthermore, a weak band at 1723 cm−1 suggests the existence of carboxylic acid groups on the CQDs' surface (Zavareh et al., 2020). Collectively, these observations strongly support successful synthesis of CQDs. The wide peak observed at 3477 cm−1 in the PVP FTIR spectrum is likely attributed to O–H stretching (Omran & Abdullah, 2021). A C–H stretching band was observed at 2953 cm⁻¹. A strong band at 1671 cm−1, indicative of CO stretching, aligns with previous PVP spectra. Additional bands at 1487 cm−1, 1458 cm−1, 1431 cm−1, and 1372 cm−1 were associated with CH bending vibrations of cyclic CH2 group, consistent with reported PVP spectra. The peak at 1288 cm−1 is associated with C–N stretching, demonstrating consistency in PVP spectrum (Kumar et al., 2014). Moreover, bending vibrations at 1020 cm−1 and 1074 cm−1, attributed to CN binding, align with previous investigations on PVP's FTIR spectrum (Zhang et al., 2011).

The observation of characteristic vibrational bands for both CS and PVP in CS-PVP spectrum provides strong evidence for successful incorporation of both polymers. Specifically, the spectrum exhibits peak at 1650 cm−1, 1419 cm−1, and 1271 cm−1, assigned to stretching vibrations of carbonyl group, aromatic benzene ring, and C = N bond (characteristic of the pyrrolidone ring) in PVP, respectively. Bands at 1650 cm⁻¹ and 1271 cm⁻¹ exhibit considerably greater intensity than the peak observed at 1419 cm⁻¹. This is likely attributed to the overlapping contributions of CO and C = N stretching vibrations, respectively, originating from both CS and PVP components (Gerami et al., 2021). Moreover, the interaction between PVP and CS is evidenced by the involvement of OH groups in PVP and NH2 or OH groups in CS, resulting in hydrogen bonding. This interaction is further supported by the shift of absorption spectra of CS's -OH and -NH groups towards lower frequencies (approximately 3351 cm−1) upon PVP addition, along with a similar low-frequency shift of the carbonyl band. These findings align with previous literature on the interplay between PVP and CS.

The FTIR spectrum of the CS-PVP-CQDs nanocomposite demonstrates a complex interplay of characteristic peaks from each constituent, reflecting the molecular interactions between these materials. The spectrum is dominated by the strong absorptions of CS and PVP, with subtle contributions from CQDs. The broad and intense peak observed around 3400 cm⁻¹ arises from overlapping OH and NH stretching vibrations polysaccharide backbone and amino groups of CS, and PVP pyrrolidone ring. Similarly, the peak around 2920 cm⁻¹ exhibits intensified peaks reflecting combined CH stretching vibrations of methylene groups (CH2) of CS-PVP, potentially with minor contributions from CQDs. The characteristic amide I and II bands of CS, present around 1650 cm⁻¹ and 1541 cm⁻¹, respectively, may overlap with the carbonyl stretching vibrations of PVP and CQDs. Subtle shifts or variations in the intensity of these peaks, compared to pure components’ spectra, suggest interactions between the CS-PVP and CQDs. For example, the peak around 1150 cm⁻¹ likely arises from CN stretching vibrations of pyrrolidone ring in PVP and CS backbone, along with potential weak peaks originating from the surface amines of the CQDs. The CO stretching peak at 1151 cm⁻¹ likely arises from CO stretching vibrations within the polysaccharide ring and -OH groups of CS, and potentially from CO stretching vibrations of functional groups such as alcohols or ethers on the surface of CQDs.

The FTIR spectrum of CS-PVP-CQDs@ DOX prominently features peaks from CS-PVP, particularly in regions associated with OH/NH stretching, CH stretching, and the amide I/II bands, as previously discussed. The presence of CQDs contributes subtle peaks in these regions, while DOX introduces additional peaks due to its aromatic rings, carbonyl groups, and amine functionalities. A broadened and intensified peak around 3370 cm⁻¹ signifies the combined contribution of -OH, -NH2, and NH stretching vibrations of all four components. The change in shape of peaks in the 2960–2850 cm⁻¹ region likely stems from the combined contribution of CH stretching of CS-PVP-CQDs and CH stretching vibrations of DOX's aromatic rings and aliphatic chains. Additionally, the peak around 1705 cm⁻¹ could encompass amide I band of CS, carbonyl (C = O) stretching of PVP and CQDs, and C = O stretching vibrations of DOX's anthracycline ring. The peak at 1650 cm⁻¹ may confirm the presence of DOX due to its aromatic ring vibrations, potentially overlapping with C = O stretching from CS-PVP. Further, variations in peak shape and intensity around 1540 cm⁻¹ suggest an overlap between the amide II band of CS, CN stretching of PVP-CQDs, and NH bending vibrations of DOX. The presence of a peak around 1270 cm⁻¹ could result from interactions between nitrogen-containing groups of the compounds, specifically the CN stretching vibrations of CS-PVP (and possibly amine groups on CQDs) and the aromatic ring of DOX. Lastly, the peak around 1150 cm⁻¹ may display overlapping peaks associated with CO stretching vibrations from CS-PVP, CQDs, and DOX's sugar moiety and anthracycline ring.

XRD analysis was employed to determine crystalline structure and properties of synthesized materials (Ragab et al., 2024). Fig. 3 presents the XRD patterns obtained for CQDs, CS, PVP, CS-PVP, CS-PVP-CQDs nanocomposite, and CS-PVP-CQDs@DOX nanocarrier. The XRD pattern of CQDs exhibits a broad peak centered around 2θ = 27.5°, corresponding to the presence of graphite-like structures, indexed to the (002) plane. This peak indicates an interlayer spacing of 0.348 nm, suggesting amorphous nature of the material (Emam & Ahmed, 2021). This observation aligns with previous reports on the amorphous phase of carbon and is attributed to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in CQDs (Karami et al., 2023; Mindivan & Göktaş, 2023). In XRD pattern of CS, a well-defined and intense peak is observed at 22.6° This peak, spanning a broad range from 10° to 30°, indicates the semicrystalline nature of the CS sample. Notably, the degree of deacetylation significantly impacts the structural arrangement and, subsequently, the crystalline properties of CS (Attar Nosrati et al., 2020). The XRD diffractograms of purified PVP show two diffuse halo peaks around 2θ values of 11.5° and 21.6°, indicating its amorphous structure. The reduction in CS-PVP band intensity ratio observed upon addition of PVP to CS is attributed to PVP's amorphous characteristics, suggesting a high degree of miscibility and interpolymer complexation (Abdouss et al., 2023). The introduction of CQDs, known for their amorphous nature, to the CS-PVP-CQDs nanocomposite results in a broader peak around 2θ = 20–30°, potentially overlapping with the CS-PVP peak, indicating successful CQD incorporation within the CS-PVP matrix. Additionally, introducing DOX, a crystalline drug, in the CS-PVP-CQDs@ DOX composite leads to slight shifts and sharpening of the existing peaks, reflecting the crystalline nature of DOX.

Figs. 4 a and b present FESEM images of the DOX-loaded CS-PVP-CQD nanocarrier. At a larger scale (Fig. 4 a), the NPs exhibit a uniform dispersion. This homogeneity in particle size and distribution is crucial for achieving precise and consistent results in drug release tests, thus enhancing their reliability. Nanocarrier morphology and size critically influence targeted DOX delivery and controlled release, essential for effective cancer therapy (Gulati et al., 2024). As shown in Figs. 4a and b, the particles predominantly exhibit a spherical shape, a common characteristic of many nanocarrier systems. While the surface appears generally smooth, there is a degree of surface roughness present, which can potentially enhance drug loading capacity due to increased surface area. This smooth surface is also advantageous for improved stability and interaction with target cells. Additionally, the particle size of the synthesized nanocomposites is shown to be <300 nm. This size is ideal for a drug delivery system that needs to diffuse between tissues, leveraging the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect (Ashrafizadeh et al., 2023). The observed morphology and size distribution are crucial for achieving precise and reliable drug release profiles and enhanced targeting accuracy.

The CS-PVP-CQDs@ DOX nanocarrier's size and zeta potential are important factors influencing its efficacy as a drug delivery platform. Hydrodynamic diameter, polydispersity index (PDI), and size distribution of NPs were assessed using DLS analysis, which accounts for the core particle, any coatings, and the solvent layer influenced by Brownian motion. Nanoparticle size significantly impacts their ability to penetrate tumor tissues and avoid rapid clearance by the immune system. Ideally, NPs for drug delivery should fall within the 50–200 nm size range, as this range facilitates the EPR effect, allowing for accumulation within tumor tissues because of their leaky vasculature. The diameter of the NPs in this study ranges from 151 nm to 315 nm, averaging 218 nm (Fig. 4 c). Nanocarrier PDI is recorded at 218 nm (PDI= 0.12), indicating a relatively monodisperse system, as PDI values below 0.3 are favorable for drug delivery. The presence of CQDs significantly influences the overall composite size. Their small size and ability to form a protective shell around the core lead to a reduction in size. However, the subsequent loading of DOX results in an increase in the composite's overall dimensions.

The zeta potential, indicative of the composite's surface charge, is derived from the combined charges of each component and indicates the NPs' stability and interaction with biological membranes. The zeta potential measures electrostatic repulsion among particles; a lower value may cause coagulation due to increased attraction, while a higher value enhances stability by preventing aggregation. The positive charge of CS originates from the protonation of amino groups (-NH2), facilitating electrostatic interactions with the negatively charged carbonyl groups (C = O) of PVP. Consequently, combination of positively charged CS and the negative charges of both PVP and CQDs promotes a near-neutral to slightly positive zeta potential, enhancing stability and minimizing the risk of premature aggregation or rapid clearance from the systemic circulation. Drug loading in this composite can occur through various mechanisms, including entrapment within the CS-PVP matrix (facilitated by hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces), electrostatic attractions between negatively charged DOX and positively charged CS, and additional binding sites provided by the CQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 4 d, the zeta potential for the CS-PVP-CQDs@ DOX is +37 mV, indicating high stability in physiological fluids without aggregation, which is essential for optimal penetration into tumor tissues, stability in the bloodstream, and efficient cellular uptake.

The efficacy of the fabricated nanocarrier, among other factors, relies significantly on both loading and entrapment efficiency. This is due to the fact that effectiveness of a drug delivery system is compromised when it exhibits poor loading capacity (Rahmani et al., 2022). Moreover, as previously mentioned, the direct administration of DOX is hindered by limited bioavailability and poor solubility. Therefore, enhancing the loading and entrapment efficiency of DOX represents a significant advancement in drug delivery techniques. Eqs. (1) and (2) were employed to determine loading and entrapment efficiencies of the drug by determining its quantity that remained unentrapped in the ethyl acetate phase. The results demonstrated notable loading efficiency of 47.75 %±2.17 and entrapment efficiency of 86.42 %±1.39, suggesting that the fabricated nanocarrier effectively encapsulates and retains a substantial amount of DOX.

Nanocarriers can be designed to release drugs in response to external stimuli, such as temperature, pH, light, sound waves, and magnetic fields (Mi, 2020). pH-triggered nanocarriers have shown great potential due to distinct acidic pH of tumor microenvironment compared to healthy tissues (Tan et al., 2023). This pH difference enables pH-responsive nanocarriers to improve anticancer drug efficacy (Le et al., 2023). In vitro drug release was investigated using a dialysis technique under physiologically relevant conditions. The experiment was performed at 37 °C, mimicking the human body temperature, and at two pH levels (7.4 and 5.4). These pH values were selected to represent the acidic environment typically found within a tumor microenvironment and the normal pH of blood, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 5 , the release kinetics demonstrated a biphasic pattern, comprising an initial burst release phase (0–12 h) and a subsequent sustained release phase. Over the first 12 h in acidic conditions (pH 5.4), approximately 34 %±0.7 and at pH=7.4, 16 %±1.6 of DOX was released from nanocarrier. This initial burst might be attributed to adsorbed drug molecules onto the surface of the nanocarriers that are readily released during the initial stages, as well as rapid diffusion due to the high concentration gradient between the interior of the nanocarriers and the surrounding medium.

Under acidic conditions (pH 5.4), the accumulated drug release proportion consistently exceeded the typical level. Specifically, the CS-PVP-CQDs@ DOX nanocarrier discharged 76 %±1.3 and 41 %±0.4 of DOX at pH 5.4 and 7.4, respectively, following a 48-hour incubation. As Chatterjee et al. (Chatterjee et al., 2025) and Prabha et al. (Prabha & Raj, 2016) reported, the drug release rate at pH 5.4 was significantly higher than at pH 7.4. The cumulative amounts of DOX released in the physiological pH and acidic pH reached about 53 %±1.2 and 87.72 %±2.1 during 72 h, and ultimately reached 60 %±1.5 and 92.25 %±0.9 during the 96 h, respectively. A significant enhancement in drug release was observed from CS-PVP-CQDs@ DOX nanocarriers under acidic conditions (pH 5.4) relative to neutral conditions (pH 7.4). This difference can be attributed to weaker hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions between PVP and CS within the nanocomposite. The amine groups become protonated at pH 5.4, leading to repulsion between them. This protonation of the amine and carboxyl groups alters the electrostatic interaction between CS and CQDs, thereby contributing to the release of the drug (Ostovar et al., 2023). These findings indicate that the CS-PVP-CQDs@ DOX nanocarrier exhibits a pH-sensitive release profile, with complete drug release occurring at the acidic pH characteristic of the tumor microenvironment. This targeted release mechanism suggests an extended circulation time and improved drug delivery efficiency. Importantly, the enhanced drug release in the cancerous environment compared to healthy tissues suggests a reduced risk of off-target toxicity to non-cancerous cells. This controlled release pattern, coupled with minimal drug release at neutral pH, minimizes systemic side effects and potentially reduces the overall drug dosage required for effective therapy.

The results obtained from the fitting of famous kinetic models (Zero-order, First-order, Higuchi, Hixson-crowell and korsmeyer-peppas) to release data at pH 5.4 and 7.4, with the quality of fit considered in terms of correlation coefficient (R2) values, are presented in Fig. 6 and Table 1 . As can be seen in Fig. 6, the best kinetic model with the closest R2 value (0.991 at pH=5.4 and 0.995 at pH=7.4) is korsmeyer-peppas kinetic model. Although the first-order kinetic model also has R2 very close to each other (0.996 at pH=5.4 and 0.985 at pH=7.4) indicating a release mechanism dominated by diffusion processes. The value of “n” in korsmeyer-peppas kinetic model between 0.4–0.8, which is classified as an anomalous transport mechanism in this model. This release mechanism is properly designed with the nanocarrier structure and components, such that there are no physical or chemical interactions between the drug and the other components. It also keeps the nanoparticle stable, preventing any unwanted changes.

To investigate anticancer potential of synthesized nanocomposites, a comprehensive cytotoxicity study was conducted using the MTT assay. The cytotoxicity of CS-PVP-CQDs@DOX, CS-PVP-CQDs, CS-PVP, CS, and free DOX against MCF-7 breast tumor cells was evaluated after 72 h of incubation, with untreated cells serving as a control (data represent mean values from triplicate experiments). Fig. 7 presents the cytotoxicity analysis, as anticipated, control group exhibited the highest cell viability, indicating the absence of any cytotoxic effect. The negligible inhibitory effect of CS alone on MCF-7 cell viability supports its suitability as a biocompatible carrier (Dubey et al., 2022). However, the incorporation of PVP and CQDs significantly enhanced the cytotoxic effect. CS-PVP and CS-PVP-CQDs platforms demonstrated approximately 5 % and 10 % reductions in cell viability, respectively, in comparison to control. This indicates that both PVP and CQDs contribute to enhanced cytotoxicity, possibly through improved cellular uptake or interaction with cellular components.

The CS-PVP-CQDs@DOX nanocarrier exhibited the most significant reduction (p˂0.001) in cell viability (around 45 %). This observation highlights the synergistic effect of CS, PVP, CQDs, and DOX within the nanocomposite. Additionally, this substantial decrease compared to free DOX (approximately 75 %) strongly suggests a synergistic effect among the components of the nanocarrier. This observation highlights the synergistic effect of CS, PVP, CQDs, and DOX within the nanocomposite. The simultaneous interaction of these components maximized the reduction of cancer cell survival, especially when the DOX was incorporated. The enhanced cellular uptake facilitated by the CS-PVP-CQD nanocarrier likely delivers a higher effective concentration of DOX to the MCF-7 cells, leading to significantly increased cytotoxicity. The presence of CQDs, even in the absence of DOX, significantly contributed to the reduction in cell viability, suggesting an independent cytotoxic effect of CQDs. This could be attributed to the CQDs' small size, which allows them to penetrate the cell membrane and induce cytotoxicity (González-Reyna et al., 2023). These findings highlight the potent cytotoxic effects of the synthesized nanocomposites, particularly when combined with the drug, and emphasize the role of CQDs in enhancing the inhibitory impact on cancer cell viability.

Fig. 8 illustrates a comprehensive analysis of cell death mechanisms in MCF-7 breast cancer cells, comparing the control group (no drug delivery platform) with the presence of individual components and the CS-PVP-CQDs@ DOX nanocarrier. Each histogram, divided into four quadrants representing necrotic cells (Q1), late apoptosis (Q2), live cells (Q3), and early apoptosis (Q4), offers valuable insights into the cell death mechanisms induced by each platform. These regions contribute an essential understanding of the mechanisms behind cell death induced by various platforms (Hanna et al., 2023). Triggering apoptosis is a highly efficient approach for eliminating cancerous cells while preserving the integrity of normal tissues (Kiraz et al., 2016). The results demonstrated a significant shift in the cell death mechanism towards apoptosis upon incorporation of DOX into the CS-PVP-CQDs nanocomposite. Compared to the control, CS alone exhibited a minimal effect on cell viability, while the other platforms displayed a decrease in live cells, reaching the lowest value with the CS-PVP-CQDs@ DOX nanocarrier. Importantly, the overall percentage of cell death through apoptosis (Q2 and Q4) was maximized in the presence of the CS-PVP-CQDs@ DOX platform, highlighting its enhanced efficacy in inducing programmed cell death. These results are in agreement with those obtained from the MTT assay and further elucidate the mechanism of action of the nanocarrier. Live cell percentages compared to the control exhibited minimal change with CS, while other platforms experienced a decline, with the lowest values seen in CS-PVP-CQDs@ DOX. Free DOX primarily induces necrosis due to its low bioavailability and solubility, leading to non-specific damage to surrounding cells. However, the incorporation of DOX into a drug delivery system effectively overcomes these limitations, enabling targeted delivery and promoting apoptosis, a more controlled and less damaging form of cell death. This shift in cell death mechanism towards apoptosis is further corroborated by flow cytometry studies conducted specifically on the CS-PVP-CQDs nanocomposite, providing additional evidence for the improved performance of the nanocarrier in inducing targeted and efficient cancer cell death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limitations, future scope and clinical implications
ce:section.ce:para: CS-PVP-CQDs nanocarrier containing DOX drug has been very popular due to the presence of CS and PVP matrix, which have features such as availability and low price. However, increasing the drug release efficiency in this matrix can help improve the drug delivery system and also the widespread use of this nanocarrier. The use of nanoparticles with higher activity can be effective in improving the drug efficiency. Also, the use of complementary tests such as in vivo clinical help to better understand the drug's performance in the body and its anticancer properties.

DOX remains a cornerstone of cancer chemotherapy; however, its clinical efficacy is significantly hampered by limitations such as low drug loading capacity, premature drug release, and non-specific toxicity toward healthy cells. This study presented a promising approach for addressing the limitations of conventional DOX delivery by developing a novel hydrogel nanocarrier with enhanced drug loading, sustained release, and pH sensitivity, leveraging the synergistic properties of CS, CQDs, and PVP. This multifunctional nanocarrier was synthesized using a double nanoemulsion (W/O/W) technique, which ensures efficient encapsulation and controlled release of DOX. The successful integration of all components and the formation of the desired nanostructure were confirmed using FTIR and XRD analyses, which provided insights into intermolecular interactions and crystallinity, respectively. Further characterization using FE-SEM and DLS revealed uniformly dispersed, spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of 218 nm, a size range optimal for cellular uptake and passive tumor targeting. The nanocarrier system also displayed a narrow polydispersity index (PDI = 0.12), indicating a monodisperse distribution, essential for consistent and reliable drug release. This optimized nanocarrier demonstrated superior drug loading capacity, achieving a remarkable loading efficiency of 47.75 % ± 2.17 and an entrapment efficiency of 86.42 % ± 1.39. These results highlight the system's ability to encapsulate a substantial amount of DOX, thereby enhancing its bioavailability and therapeutic potential. In vitro release studies further revealed a sustained and pH-responsive release profile. At pH 5.4, mimicking the acidic tumor microenvironment, 92.25 % ± 0.9 % of the DOX was released over 96 h, while at physiological pH 7.4, only 60 % ± 1.5 % was released over the same period. This pH-responsive behavior ensures targeted drug delivery to tumor tissues while minimizing off-target effects and reducing systemic toxicity. The enhanced therapeutic efficacy of the CS-PVP-CQDs@DOX nanocarrier was validated through in vitro cytotoxicity and apoptosis assays, demonstrating significantly increased cancer cell death compared to free DOX. This superior performance is attributed to the synergistic interaction between CS, PVP, CQDs, and DOX within the nanocomposite facilitating improved cellular uptake and targeted delivery, leading to enhanced cytotoxicity and apoptosis induction in cancer cells. The combined effect of these components resulted in enhanced cellular internalization and targeted delivery of DOX, leading to increased cytotoxicity and apoptosis induction in breast cancer cells. These findings indicated the potential of the developed nanocarrier to achieve more targeted drug delivery and increased antitumor efficacy and could provide a noteworthy advancement in the field of cancer nanomedicine through introducing a promising novel method for targeted delivery of DOX, with the potential to improve treatment outcomes and minimize adverse effects in breast cancer patients.

Soheil Abdollahi nohooji: Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Azam Ghadami: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Software, Investigation, Data curation. Mehrab Pourmadadi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Fatemeh Yazdian: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Data curation. Ali Kazemi: Visualization, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mojdeh Mirshafiei: Writing – original draft, Validation, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis. Soheyl Hojaty Bastami: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis.
